It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Judicial enforcement deputies: Causes and effects of Chinese judges enforcing environmental administrative decisions Xuehua Zhang Institute of New Energy and Low Carbon Technology, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, China Abstract This paper explores how and why Chinese courts became involved in regulating pollution by aiding administrative agencies in executing sanction decisions and collecting pollution levies. It also studies the effects of their regulatory involvement, both in terms of deterrence inferred from available information, as well as the compliance behavior of regulated actors deﬁned as payment of regulatory penalties. It ﬁnds that judicial involvement in regulatory enforcement proved to be short-lived and depended on a particular context at a particular period of time when a regulatory need coincided with administrative and ﬁnancial judicial needs and particular judges. The paper also ﬁnds that court involvement enhanced deterrence in terms of certainty of punishment and to some extent the severity of punishment; however that deterrent effect was undermined by the close relationship between the courts and the regulated entities as ﬁnes and levies were almost always negotiated. In effect, deterrence increased the number of compliers paying levies without increasing the depth of compliance in terms of their full payment. Keywords: administrative litigation law, compliance, courts, deterrence, enforcement, regulation. 1. Introduction In recent years, research on the bilateral regulatory process that merely involves the regulators versus the regulated has grown into interest in research on regulations comprising a more multifaceted regulatory landscape involving multiple actors (Grabosky 1995; Gunningham et al. 1998; Black 2001; Black 2002; Gunningham & Sinclair 2002; Black 2003; Gunningham et al. 2003; Grabosky 2013). This new approach, known as “decentered” regulation, has largely focused on new market and social actors. Examples are Grabosky’s (2013) study on the use of technology to enhance the regulatory function of non-state actors; the study by Cherney et al. (2006) on how dense networks and institutions assisted police efforts to reduce the supply of illicit synthetic drugs; and Van Rooij’s (2012) work examining the role of Chinese citizens in risk regulations. However, little attention has been paid to courts acting as regulatory authorities (a notable exception is Thiruvengadam & Joshi 2012).1 In the context of Chinese environmental regulation, existing studies have predominantly focused on the con- ventional state regulators, that is, environmental agencies (Jahiel 1994, 1997; Chan et al. 1995; Sinkule & Ortolano 1995; Wong & Chan 1996; Ma & Ortolano 2000; Zhang 2001; Lo & Fryxell 2003, 2005; Tang et al. 2003; Van Rooij 2003; Warwick 2003; Lo et al. 2006; Tilt 2007). Some recent studies have examined civil society including ordinary Chinese citizens (Brettell 2003; Warwick 2003; Wang et al. 2004; Tilt 2007; Zhang 2010; Van Rooij 2012), but very few studies have examined the role of courts in environmental regulation. The only study I am aware of is the paper by Zhang et al. (2010) that analyzes the legal processes and outcomes of court enforcement cases ﬁled by environmental agencies under the Administrative Litigation Law (ALL) promulgated in 1989 and the effects of that court enforce- ment on agencies. Article 66 stipulates that administrative regulators can ﬁle court cases if the regulated parties fail to comply with administrative orders and that citizens can initiate legal action against regulators. The legislative history and the wording of the ALL suggest that protecting citizens’ interests via litigation cases is the primary objective; assisting bureaucracies in exercising their authority via execution cases is a supplementary function (Potter 1991; Zhang 1991; Marshall 2003). Rather than examining outcomes from the perspective of the ALL and the development of legal practices under it, this paper takes a different approach. It is an empirical case study of the involvement of Chinese courts in environmental regulations. Speciﬁcally, this paper seeks to explain: (i) why courts started acting as judicial enforce- ment deputies of environmental regulations; and (ii) how court involvement affected the behavior of regulated parties and regulations in terms of compliance with their legal obligation to pay pollution levies. The analysis in this paper combines a multi-actor, decentered approach to regulation with a study of how this affects deterrence and compliance with the legal obligation to pay pollution levies. Increasingly, the regulatory literature agrees that successful regulation requires multi-actor involvement beyond traditional administrative regulators (Black 2003). This paper ﬁnds that the involvement of judges in regulatory enforcement can occur when certain incentives are in place and when regulatory-oriented judges manage the court; however, the incentives and the judges may change, and, as such, judicial involvement in regulation can be uncertain and short-lived, as in the cases studied herein. In terms of regulatory effects, this paper ﬁnds that although judicial involvement enhanced both the perceived certainty of punishment and, to a certain degree, the severity of sanctions, it did not end the practice of negotiating payments of levies and, thus, achieved only partial compliance with limited regulatory effects. A broader lesson here is that deterrence can enhance the number of ﬁrms complying (paying any pollution levies at all) without enhancing the depth of compliance (the extent to which ﬁrms paid the full amount). The primary data in this paper are archival court records and interviews collected from Hubei Province from April 2005 to July 2006.2 Two rural counties (counties B and C) were selected for in-depth interviews as they were regarded as the most effective courts in implementing the ALL. I also chose a district of Wuhan City (District A), the capital of Hubei Province, to provide an urban context for this analysis. In China, both counties and city districts enjoy the same administrative rank. Interviews with environmental ofﬁcials, judges, and polluters were conducted. The selection of three counties in Hubei was not intended to be representative of all situations nationally; it was appropriate because there are limited empirical studies on the regulatory effects of decentered regulation in China. This exploratory study contributes empirically to the ﬁelds of decentered regulation and deterrence theory, albeit with clear limitations. First of all, it primarily studies compliance with the legal obligation to pay pollution levies and mentions brieﬂy whether compliance reduced pollution or improved environmental quality. Second, it studies the subjective deterrence of relevant ﬁrms and local actors using available evidence from interviews. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 explains why courts became judicial enforcement deputies for environmental agencies. Section 3 discusses the deterrent effects of court involvement on the behavior of the regulated parties and the factors leading to partial compliance. Section 4 concludes the paper with implications. 2. Preconditions for regulatory involvement A vital question related to courts as environmental regulators is why they took on regulatory roles in the ﬁrst place. According to Black (2003), authority and legitimacy are well recognized as key sources contributing to the capacity of new actors to regulate. As the discussion in this section shows, Chinese courts have both the authority and legitimacy granted by the ALL to help enforce environmental decisions issued by local environmental agencies. As a result, the courts and environmental agencies formed a symbiotic exchange that was, according to Liu (2011), inherently two-sided, mutually contingent, and mutually rewarding. This incentive-based entry of the courts onto the environmental regulatory landscape is further demonstrated by the decline in court cases ﬁled by environmental agencies when the incentives were partially removed. 2.1. Regulatory need Environmental agencies in China are known as weak regulatory agencies with limited enforcement power (Jahiel 1994, 1997; Marshall 2003; Zhang 2004; Van Rooij 2006, 2012; Tilt 2007; Van Rooij et al. 2010). Although they can Judicial enforcement deputies X. Zhang © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 30 Page 3: impose administrative penalties, they do not have the power to force compliance.3 When authorized by related laws, the courts can apply a series of compulsory measures when handling cases, for example, sequestering, detaining, freezing, or selling off the property of a party and detaining that party for a maximum of 15 days.4 In contrast, the most an environmental agency can do on its own is to order a violator to stop production until the pollution is under control or to issue a penalty decision.When the violator ignores the order, the environmental agency cannot take any further action to bring the violator into compliance. The Vice Director of an environmental agency I interviewed vividly describes this situation: Resistance to environmental enforcement at the local level was tremendous. Environmental agencies did not have coercive power and could issue only a penalty document. That an environmental penalty decision became a meaningless piece of paper was very common.5 With weak regulatory power, local environmental agencies encountered massive resistance to paying pollution levies and related penalties when China implemented the system in the late 1970s.6 Resistance to paying pollution levies became more pronounced among small, tertiary industries when the Hubei provincial regulations issued in 1994 and 1995 expanded the scope of the levy system from enterprises and service units to “. . . all units and individual industrial commercial businesses” (Hubei Provincial People’s Congress 1994; Hubei Provincial Govern- ment 1995). This included small and individual businesses in the tertiary sector. Individual business owners and small businesses did not understand why they needed to pay pollution levies if they did not generate any pollution or if their pollution was minimal.7 The owners of many barbershops asked, “Don’t you wash your hair at home? Have your families ever paid pollution levies?”8 A more general question was how they could create pollution if they were not enterprises. Thus, levy collection by environmental agencies had little legitimacy in the eyes of the regulated parties. While collecting levies remained a signiﬁcant challenge, the funds collected had, nevertheless, become the major source of revenue for environmental agencies. Many local governments in less developed regions that had not been able to collect sufﬁcient tax revenue to meet their obligations to provide public goods and services were unable to provide the funding environmental agencies needed to operate. As the agencies depended on local governments for funding, personnel, arrangements, and beneﬁts, they focused their enforcement efforts on collecting levies in order to generate revenue for survival and growth (Jahiel 1994, 1997; Sinkule & Ortolano 1995; Ma & Ortolano 2000; Van Rooij 2003; Zhang 2010). Because they lacked the authority and coercive power to force compliance when polluters refused to pay levies and related penalties, some local environmental agencies, such as those analyzed in this study, turned to the courts for assistance. 2.2. Administrative judicial beneﬁts While the ﬁrst incentive came from environmental agencies that needed court assistance in executing their penalty decisions, a second main incentive came from courts that needed execution cases to fulﬁll ALL-related caseload requirements. Soon after the ALL went into effect in 1990, all local courts were under pressure to increase the numbers of ALL cases in order to fulﬁll the expectations of superior units.9 Their workloads affected the perfor- mance evaluations of local courts and of individual judges. A number of judges in this study indicated that the workload on “administrative cases” at that time was based on a combination of two types of lawsuits ﬁled under the ALL: (i) administrative litigation cases ﬁled by ordinary citizens and organizations against administrative agencies; and (ii) court execution cases ﬁled by administrative agencies requesting court enforcement of their decisions.10 An administrative division was established within a court to handle ALL-related cases. Not surprisingly, many courts in the early 1990s had very few litigation cases, which made fulﬁlling the ALL caseload requirements difﬁcult. This was certainly true in the three localities in this study (see Table 1). The concept of “citizens suing ofﬁcials” was alien to ordinary Chinese people. The historical and traditional idea was that common people could not sue an ofﬁcial and win in court.11 Very few people dared to challenge a government agency or ofﬁcial. This longstanding unwillingness of individuals in Chinese society to challenge ofﬁcial power has been mentioned by a number of Chinese senior judges and scholars (Huang 1991; Jiang 1998; Hung 2001; Peerenboom 2002; Marshall 2003; Heuser 2003). People believe that winning a lawsuit could require losing one’s life (Huang 1991).12 Judicial enforcement deputies X. Zhang © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 31 Page 4: Lack of an adequate ALL caseload appeared to threaten even the existence of the administrative divisions of some courts. The administrative division chief of a municipal court in Hubei Province recalled an instance in 1991 in which the division of a district court was almost entirely eliminated because it failed to increase the number of both litigation and execution cases.13 Liu (2006), in reporting on the scarcity of litigation cases at a county court, observed that many activities other than case trials were created to legitimize the existence of the administrative division. This investigation found that judges in County B were engaged in extensive pre-trial investigations, such as interviewing all individuals involved in each litigation case in order to create work for themselves.14 While it was difﬁcult, if not impossible, for courts to increase their ALL caseloads by getting ordinary citizens to ﬁle more litigation cases, many judges mentioned that aggressive outreach by courts to agencies to generate court execution cases could be easily orchestrated.15 Such court–agency collaboration was efﬁciently accomplished using the widespread practice of establishing “circuit courts” within various government agencies throughout the nation. Each of the three local courts in this study installed a circuit court at the environmental agency. The following recollection of a senior judge at a municipal court in Hubei Province describes their purpose and operation: Circuit courts were established to promote administrative adjudication work. At that time, there were too few lawsuits. In order to expand administrative adjudication work, good local innovations emerged. In 1991, our court president led a team to learn from Pingdingshan City [about circuit courts]. After the president returned, responding to the demand of agencies, we chose to set up circuit courts in the environmental agency, the land bureau, the planning bureau, and the water bureau . . . I was assigned to work with the environmental agency.16 The above remarks demonstrate the scarcity of administrative litigation cases ﬁled under the ALL in the early 1990s. Local courts regarded the establishment of administrative “circuit courts” as an innovative local practice that could increase the number of execution cases ﬁled by administrative agencies. It appears that agencies also expressed a desire to use court authority to assist them in their work. The interviewee indicated that circuit courts were ﬁrst established in the designated agencies because, like the environmental agencies, each of them had administrative enforcement responsibilities but limited power. The China Environmental News, the most prominent national environmental newspaper, reported the emer- gence and spread of environmental circuit courts throughout the nation from 1991 to 1995. Approximately 40 news articles were written during that period on circuit courts established at local environmental agencies in 16 provinces. 2.3. Financial judicial beneﬁts The courts also had economic incentives to become judicial enforcement deputies for administrative agencies as court execution cases could bring in signiﬁcant revenue. An environmental ofﬁcial and a polluter each used similar language to talk about the relationship between courts and environmental agencies.17 As one said, “Courts and environmental agencies are a proﬁtable community. Both make money by executing [court cases].”18 For the courts, the funds that were collected from enforcing pollution levy decisions generated substantial income;19 this became an important source of revenue because of the general lack of sufﬁcient funding at the county level nationally. In principle, courts should be fully funded by local governments; in reality, many county courts in less-developed regions do not always receive sufﬁcient funds to cover their expenditures.20 The inadequate funding of local courts is a well-documented problem (Li 2004; He 2012). As previously mentioned, county governments often lack sufﬁcient tax revenue and, thus, are unable to provide enough funds for administrative agencies. To cover beneﬁts and bonuses, the courts, which are treated as adminis- trative agencies, have to rely on court fees for handling cases, such as court execution cases. Each division (or even each individual judge in some localities) was assigned a caseload quota; the degree to which it was ﬁlled was linked to the bonus.22 According to a court ofﬁcial in County B,“Allocating caseload quotas to each adjudication division – civil, criminal and economic – has been very common in Hubei Province.”23 Monetary gains from execution cases for the courts became more pronounced after the ﬁrst ones were ﬁled by environmental agencies. In all three localities in this study, courts received processing fees from polluters taken to court. In addition, with the environmental agencies’ full agreement, courts were sometimes able to keep additional processing fees that the agencies paid in advance with the expectation that they would be repaid later.24 The environmental agencies in counties B and C sometimes even let courts keep the levies they collected to provide additional incentives to support levy collection in the future.25 The critical role that economic gains from execution cases played in court involvement in administrative enforcement is well summarized by the then Vice President of the Hubei Provincial Court: At the beginning of the ALL, administrative divisions received few administrative litigation cases. They took the initiative to approach administrative agencies and work for agencies . . . Court enforcement of agency decisions could bring economic beneﬁts to both courts and environmental agencies. Thus, the two parties came to a consensus that led to the initiation of court enforcement of agency decisions.26 It has been well documented that Chinese courts function within the requirements introduced by the central authority; as a result, the incentive mechanism has played a key role in shaping court work and behavior (Fu & Cullen 2011; Ng & He 2013). When the incentives were partially removed later, the number of court execution cases involving pollution levies declined substantially. 2.4. Decline of court involvement and the removal of incentives Figure 1 demonstrates that the number of environmental execution cases declined signiﬁcantly after reaching a peak in 1999 in District A and in 2002 in County C and stayed relatively ﬂat thereafter.27 Reduced court involvement was largely a result of the partial removal of the incentives that propelled courts to enter the environmental regulatory ﬁeld in the ﬁrst place. As interviewees in both District A and County C noted, the most obvious one was that court enforcement effectively increased the payment of pollution levies and, thus, reduced the need for court interven- tion.28 Many polluters came to realize that they would have to pay levies eventually and would be better off if they negotiated acceptable amounts with environmental agencies before going to court. Also, during the course of court enforcement, environmental ofﬁcials came to know many polluters well and cultivated guanxi (connections) with many business owners.29 As long as polluters did not completely refuse to pay levies and were willing to negotiate, environmental agencies would avoid issuing formal penalty decisions that could ruin their relationships.30 With 0 50 100 150 200 1992 1993 1994 1995 1996 1997 1998 1999 2000 2001 2002 2003 2004 2005 Number of Cases Year County C District A Figure 1 Number of court cases ﬁled by environmental agencies in District A (1992–2005) and County C (1995–2005), Hubei Province. It gradually increased and reached a peak. By the time the peak was reached, court enforcement had already had signiﬁcant effects [on polluters]. Many people recognized the environmental agency’s authority and began cooperating with it. The agency did not need us anymore. Therefore, the number of cases has fallen subsequently.32 Some environmental ofﬁcials and court judges in District A believed that removing ALL-related caseload pressure also contributed to the decline in court execution cases since 2001 in that district.33 In 2003, the munici- pal court supervising the District A Court stopped evaluating lower court performance based on the combined number of litigation and execution cases;34 greater emphasis was placed on litigation cases. Another possible explanation for the reduced role of the courts was their more stringent review of execution cases, which made environmental agencies more reluctant to ﬁle them. This is reﬂected in District A Court’s 2004 requirement for informal hearings involving regulated parties and agencies to identify (mostly) procedural errors that could not be determined by examining agency documents.35 Some environmental personnel considered such informal hearings as “quasi-court investigations,”36 and others complained that many cases were rejected for minor errors.37 The factors discussed above do not, however, explain why the declines took place earlier in District A than in County C. To understand this, we need to look at changes in court staff in the district.38 Three key court staff – the president, the administrative division chief, and a senior judge – who were instrumental in initiating and executing environmental cases all left the court or administrative division in 2000.39 The strong, personal court–agency relationship for active court enforcement of environmental penalty decisions no longer existed. The new court president and administrative division chief who both had strong legal training backgrounds appeared to be more responsive to the policy change that removed caseload pressure; they instead placed more emphasis on litigation cases and were inclined to strengthen reviews of execution cases.40As the new court leaders decided to adopt those changes after 2000, the court procedures for handling execution cases in District A became more stringent, complex, and lengthy in the eyes of environmental ofﬁcials. Analogous changes in court staff were not, however, found in County C before the number of execution cases declined notably in 2003. As this study investigated three localities and declines took place in only two of them, I cannot generalize that the deterrent effect of court enforcement in terms of the increased certainty of punishment was the most important factor in the declines. What I can argue is that the partial removal of the incentives that propelled the courts into the environmental regulatory ﬁeld led to a decline in court enforcement of environmental regulation in later years. All of this shows how fragile court involvement in environmental regulation can be. Involvement premised on regulatory needs and judicial beneﬁts, as well as on particular judges, can change as those needs, beneﬁts, and judges change, demonstrating the potential for inconsistency from actors whose core mission is not regulatory. Moreover, although the primary goal of environmental regulation is to control pollution and to maintain or improve local environmental quality, court involvement in environmental enforcement work did not appear to achieve that goal as it primarily targeted small, tertiary industries that discharged very small amounts of pollution. The environmental agencies were not willing to take large, non-compliant enterprises to court, nor could courts enforce their decisions even if the agencies did so (Zhang et al. 2010). Intervention from local government leaders eager to protect those large enterprises – the main sources of local revenue – from strict environmental regulation was the key reason for the lack of court action. 3. Regulatory effects In theory, court involvement in executing administrative sanctions could enhance compliance by both enhancing the certainty as well as the legitimacy of punishments. Existing subjective deterrence literature has consistently found that certainty of punishment is more inﬂuential in achieving compliant behavior than the severity of punishment (e.g. Williams & Hawkins 1986; Nagin 2013). In this case, the courts may have helped to increase the certainty of Judicial enforcement deputies X. Zhang © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 34 Page 7: punishment by increasing the likelihood that those sanctioned for breaking the law would actually ﬁnd that those sanctions were executed. The data presented show that court involvement indeed enhanced the certainty and even the severity of sanctions to a certain extent; however, the regulatory effects were limited as close relationships and judicial discretion helped to maintain the practices of negotiating payments and of partial compliance with very limited effects on reducing actual pollution. Court involvement increased the number of ﬁrms paying pollution levies and related penalties, but, as explained in the second part of this section, it did not necessarily increase the amount of levies and penalties paid. It is important to note that compliance here refers primarily to paying pollution levies and related penalties. I do not have sufﬁcient information to assess whether ﬁrms complied with environmental standards as a result of court involvement, although local environmental quality did not notably improve when courts enforced environmental decisions. Moreover, the deterrent effect discussed here is largely based on interviews with judges, environmental agency staff, and polluters subjected to court actions; they demonstrate that particular speciﬁc and general deter- rence existed and affected levy payments. Based on these interviews, I suggest relevant lessons, but whether they would also apply beyond the cases discussed is a matter for further study. 3.1. Certainty and severity of sanctions As discussed previously, in China, courts, and not environmental agencies, have the legal authority to enforce environmental sanctions when a regulated party refuses to comply. The interviews revealed that when courts started to collaborate with environmental agencies to execute their decisions, deterrence appeared to increase. Several interviewees indicated that as soon as judges appeared in front of a polluter and announced a deadline for paying the levies and sometimes delivered a court summons in person, polluters realized that the courts were backing up the agencies and that pollution levies needed to be paid; many of them chose to approach the agencies and to negotiate a reduced levy.41 In these cases, court execution enhanced the perceived deterrent effect on polluting ﬁrms and enhanced the certainty of punishment (Becker 1968; Nagin 2013). Court involvement did not improve detection or even the probability that a sanction would be issued, but rather enhanced the execution of sanctions already issued to ﬁrms in violation. Court involvement also may have enhanced speciﬁc deterrence by increasing the severity of expected punish- ments and, in addition, could create extra costs as ﬁrms would also have to pay court fees (Nagin 2013). As a small restaurant owner in County C said,“I paid both pollution levies and court fees. The court can temporarily shut down your business if you do not pay. You know you need to pay once judges come. Many people felt the same way.”42 By negotiating a mutually agreeable levy, polluters could avoid paying court fees. Several judges and environmental ofﬁcials mentioned that polluters who were subjected to court action became more compliant in paying pollution levies in subsequent years;43 this is supported by court statistics that recurrent actions involving the same polluter were rare and that most offenders were polluters who had not previously experienced court action. Court statistics indicated that 12 out of 360 cases (3 percent) in District A, ﬁve out of 102 cases (5 percent) in County B, and 35 out of 856 cases (4 percent) in County C were recurrent. Moreover, most non-compliant cases were new owners of the same business (e.g. new president of a county agricultural bank) who refused to pay,44 and construction companies with multiple sites.45 A judge mentioned that occasionally a polluter might refuse to pay again because the environmental agency had raised the levy when the agency–polluter relation- ship had temporarily broken down.46 The interviewees also related cases in which court actions seem to have had a general deterrent effect on the regulated community as a whole, particularly on parties that were initially not willing to pay levies and penalties, but had not been taken to court (Thornton et al. 2005; Nagin 2013). The courts could invoke strong measures, such as placing polluters in custody for as many as 15 days and conﬁscating their private property, although they took such actions only when polluters refused to cooperate and acted poorly toward the judges. For example, in 1996, the County C court conﬁscated a jeep from a township food management station that refused to pay a pollution levy of 1,000 RMB.47 The station had to pay a total of 8,000 RMB, including a ﬁne and court fees, in order to get the jeep back. As an environmental ofﬁcial, whose aunt-in-law was the vice director of the county food management bureau, recalled, the bureau briefed all of its subsidiaries about the incident, and since then the subsidiaries no longer openly resisted pollution levies.48 Instead, they negotiated a lower levy emphasizing their difﬁculty in paying the full amount and, thus, avoided paying court fees.49 Judicial enforcement deputies X.
