Several court policemen in a patrol vehicle were sent to detain the owner of a small restaurant who refused to pay a levy and even threw away the court summons in front of the judges who delivered it to the restaurant.50 The owner had to write self-criticism while handcuffed in front of the public. The envi- ronmental agency invited a local TV station to report on the incident and to broadcast that report frequently afterwards.51 More than 100 people dining in the restaurant or in nearby restaurants witnessed the proceedings. The vice director of the environmental agency recalled that he heard a woman talking about this incident in a farm market and telling her friends, “Do not throw away environmental documents. Otherwise the court can detain you.”52 The general deterrent effects in these cases are nicely summarized by environmental and court interviewees using a well-known Chinese idiom, which is literally translated as “Kill one to scare a hundred.”53 In County C, four out of seven polluters clearly recalled that many small businesses were taken to court when they refused to pay levies.54 They had also heard of incidents of detaining a person or of conﬁscating property. In County B, two polluters talked about the small restaurant owner who was detained by the court in 2005.55 According to one interviewee, the environmental agency threatened that,“Whoever refuses to pay pollution levies from now on will be detained by the court [like the restaurant owner].”56 An important factor strengthening the general deterrent effect in these cases was the nature of the regulatory ﬁeld and of the localities as it helped to disseminate information about sanctions. All three were small societies, and the comfortable relationship between the courts and the regulated parties in them meant that court execution cases and coercive measures were widely known among the regulated parties which encouraged otherwise non- cooperative polluters to work with environmental agencies. As shown in section 3.2, however, those same close relationships also appeared to undermine the deterrent effect as pollution levies and penalties were never paid in full as required by environmental regulations. 3.2. Partial compliance: The inﬂuence of close relationships and judicial discretion A second key ﬁnding of this study is that although court involvement helped to enhance subjective deterrence in particular cases, its effects on compliance in terms of paying pollution levies and penalties and on reducing pollution remained disappointing. The analysis illustrates that the close relationships between the regulated and the regulators in the small localities combined with broad judicial discretion enabled widespread negotiations that resulted in the partial payment of levies and penalties and, thus, in partial compliance. This shows that in the cases studied, enhanced deterrence may increase the number of ﬁrms that comply, but may not necessarily improve the level of payment in each case. The close relationship between judges and local factory owners greatly inﬂuenced what the factories ultimately had to pay. While close relationships and small community size improved the dissemination of court actions and penalties, they also undermined regulatory effects. In these small localities, traditional guanxi (social relations) and renqing (human feelings) heavily penetrate almost every aspect of society and the lives of local people. At the heart of guanxi and renqing is the notion of reciprocal obligation and indebtedness. Nearly every judge and environmental ofﬁcial I interviewed mentioned the extensive networks of local guanxi and renqing in their localities and the pressure they put on their work.57 An environmental ofﬁcial in District A said: Requesting a favor and looking for guanxi are extremely common. This is a Chinese characteristic. Once something happens, the ﬁrst thing a person does is to look for guanxi and renqing. The course of court enforcement is full of renqing.58 Most judges and environmental ofﬁcials were born and grew up in the localities in which they work. During the course of schooling, marriage, work, and other social activities, they established and maintained their guanxi and possessed (or owed) enormous amounts of renqing. A County C court judge who had been in charge of execution cases ﬁled by the environmental agency for over a decade provided a speciﬁc example that portrays the guanxi and renqing pressure a judge is subjected to at work: In my heart, I did not want or dare to take court execution cases. County C is such a small place. My work involved several hundreds of businesses each year. I feel I have offended almost everyone. Once when I went to Judicial enforcement deputies X. Zhang © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 36 Page 9: XX hotpot restaurant, the owner stared at me for quite a while and looked very guarded. I had to explain that I did not come for work today. I just came to have dinner. I felt very awkward.59 The profound inﬂuence of guanxi and renqing is well documented. For example, Li (2011) explains that guanxi embodies certain rules and codes of conduct that purport to remove the legal, moral, and cognitive barriers impeding the contracting process of corrupt exchanges. In the cases studied here, the enormous pressures of guanxi and renqing forced judges to compromise when doing their work. As the vice director of the environmental agency in County C indicated: Judges are local people and have a lot of local guanxi and renqing, and thus they cannot fully enforce [the high fees] either. When judges could not process a case because of guanxi and renqing, they came to negotiate with us in private while persuading the polluter to pay some of the levy.60 During this process, judges often acted as mediators who either suggested that polluters directly negotiate a deal with the environmental agencies or negotiated reduced levies with the agencies on behalf of polluters.61 One judge in County C provided a detailed account of the tremendous pressure of guanxi and renqing and how he dealt with it: I have spent at least 50 percent of my energy on handling enormous amounts of renqing involved in my work. People asked us for a favor. We then consulted with the environmental agency. For example, you may know my brother and come to ask me for a favor. I can call to tell the environmental agency that you are my relative or friend and ask for a reduction. Generally, the agency listens to me. Meanwhile, I cannot violate the principles to exempt you from any levy because such an exemption is illegal. I will show you legal provisions and make you understand that you are legally obligated to pay a pollution levy. I know the environmental agency usually collects 200 RMB in a situation like yours; the agency might have asked you to pay 600 RMB.You may negotiate with the agency directly to get your levy reduced to 200 RMB, or I can step forward to help you negotiate with the agency. In addition, court fees include a ﬁxed fee per case and actual court expenses. I can waive your actual court expenses. In this way, I will not offend everyone while doing my work.62 This judge needed to maintain his guanxi or repay his renqing, so he managed to give a discount on the levies and/or penalties. As environmental ofﬁcials in all three study areas indicated, judges know how much of the levies agencies expected to collect (or had actually collected in the past), that is, the bottom line in the interviewees’ own words. Thereby, judges often did favors for polluters at little cost to themselves by simply mediating a mutually acceptable bottom line.63 Indeed, court mediation in getting levies and related penalties reduced and/or waived was a common practice although the ALL does not explicitly permit court mediation in handling ALL cases, including execution cases.64 Consequently, as nearly every judge and environmental ofﬁcial interviewed claimed, pollution levies were rarely collected in full.65 A small portion of the available court records on execution cases ﬁled by environmental agencies showed that of the 77 cases ﬁled in District A from 1995 to 2000, there were only eight cases (about 10 percent) in which 100 percent of the levies and penalties requested by the agency were actually collected. In the remaining 69, the average amount collected was 23.5 percent, and in approximately two thirds, less than 20 percent was collected. In County B, the situation was more astonishing. None of the seven cases in 2003 paid 100 percent of the levies and ﬁnes issued: the highest was 20 percent, the lowest was ﬁve percent, and the average was 10.5 percent. It is important to note that individual judges derived signiﬁcant discretionary power from internal court procedures, and that enabled them to mediate. For example, before 2001, District A court’s administrative division alone accepted, examined, and executed all court execution cases.66 One or two judges were in charge of reviewing cases, while another one or two were in charge of execution. Upon receiving applications from agencies, judges examined the documents and notiﬁed the agencies within seven days whether or not the applications were approved. If substantial errors were found, they returned the applications to the agencies to ﬁx the problems and reapply for court execution. Approved cases were then given to the judges in charge of execution who notiﬁed the parties involved and demanded voluntary compliance within 15 days or compulsory enforcement would be applied. If any errors were found during execution, the judges could ask the agencies either to make corrections and reapply or to drop the case.67 Judicial enforcement deputies X. Zhang © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 37 Page 10: All of this demonstrates that the close relationship between the regulators and the regulated combined with broader discretion could enhance deterrence, but not necessarily lead to better compliance in terms of levy and penalty payments. This affects the outcome of regulation as the levies paid in the study area remained too low to induce reductions in pollution. Getting caught and punished does not necessarily have much effect if compliance can be negotiated. Perhaps in conditions such as those in China, deterrence should be viewed in the context of how regulators and the regulated come to deﬁne what constitutes compliance, which, in this case, meant a negotiated payment of levies, rather than a full payment. Therefore, when studying a clearly exogenous element of compliance, namely the effects of punishment on rule abiding behavior, we may have to draw from endogenous approaches to compliance that analyze how regulatory interaction shapes the meaning of what is seen as compliance (Parker & Nielsen 2009). 4. Conclusions and implications This analysis of the entry of new actors onto the regulatory environmental landscape generates mixed ﬁndings. It requires a particular historical combination of incentives for the administrative agency and for the judiciary to create a mutually beneﬁcial collaboration. Environmental agencies lack the authority to enforce their sanctions when polluters refuse to pay while the courts have the legal authority to execute cases ﬁled by regulatory agencies to fulﬁll their caseload targets under the ALL. Moreover, enforcing environmental sanctions brought notable revenue to the courts. It was the combination of these three factors that propelled courts to enter the environmental regulatory ﬁeld and to become judicial enforcement deputies for environmental agencies soon after the ALL came into effect in the 1990s. It is not surprising then that the role of the courts in environmental regulations diminished when the incentives changed in the 2000s. Court enforcement of administrative sanctions had a double-edged effect on environmental regulation. While the close relationship between the regulated (polluters) and the regulators (environmental agencies and courts) in the form of traditional guanxi/renqing enhanced speciﬁc and general deterrence in particular cases, as demonstrated in the interviews, it led to only partial compliance as pollution levies continued to be negotiated and were rarely collected in full. Primarily targeting court enforcement on small polluters generating little pollution did not lead to notable pollution reduction or to improved local environmental quality, the principle objectives of environmental regulation. Broad discretion and close relationships led to negotiations about what constituted compliance, which appeared to be the partial payment of pollution levies and penalties, rather than full payment.As a result, in the cases studied, the eventual effects of enhanced deterrence were watered down as lower levels of payment were accepted. The courts’ involvement in executing administrative sanction decisions did not come without cost as their regulatory functions appeared to start to undermine their intended judicial function of adjudicating citizen–state disputes. In the two courts studied, their execution of administrative sanctions dwarfed their regular administrative adjudication work, especially when they were most active as enforcement deputies. In the three localities where this ﬁeld research took place, an even greater discrepancy occurred: Nearly 99 percent of the ALL cases involving environmental agencies from 1992 to 2005 were court execution cases ﬁled by agencies against polluters, while only one percent were litigation cases ﬁled by citizens and polluters against agencies.68 Although departures from the overall pattern exist, it is reasonable to conclude that courts had become primarily judicial enforcement deputies for environmental regulations, not for protecting citizens’ rights. The ﬁndings of this paper have several implications for studying the regulatory landscape and for the role of deterrence on compliance behavior (here deﬁned as the payment of pollution levies and penalties). First, we need more empirical work exploring the conditions under which new actors might be enrolled in regulation. Black (2003) has identiﬁed six key resources as critical to the performance of regulatory functions: information, expertise, ﬁnancial and economic resources, authority and legitimacy, strategic position, and organizational capacity. This study of courts acting as environmental enforcement deputies demonstrates that authority and legitimacy are key resources contributing to the capacity of both the state regulators (environmental agencies) and non-state actors (courts) to regulate; moreover, both faced notable incentives to enroll new authorities into the regulatory ﬁeld and to form an interdependent relationship. This incentive-based enrollment enhanced their regulatory capacity. As Black (2003) indicates, the potential for interdependency to increase overall capacity is overlooked and needs further empirical investigation. Judicial enforcement deputies X. Subjective deterrence theory predicts that it is the perceived likelihood of detection that changes the behavior of the regulated (Becker 1968; Thornton et al. 2005; Parker & Nielsen 2009). Within the limited conﬁnes of the cases studied here, we can carefully hypothesize that deterrence might also require that any sanctions issued are actually executed. This is not new to deterrence theory as it is assumed in the idea of certainty and severity of punishment that the punishment is actually meted out. Most studies, however, do not focus on the actual execution of punishment. A notable exception is Ross and Pritikin (2010) who have highlighted that, even in the United States, there are many instances where ﬁnes and penalties for corporate and white-collar crimes remain underpaid or even uncollected. In China, where the actual execution of judicial and administrative decisions has long been known to be an ongoing problem,69 regulatory scholarship must look beyond the chance of being caught and the severity of punishment to the certainty that punishment is actually meted out. Third, the cases reported in this study may illustrate that in a context where there is a close relationship and where there is legal or factual discretion, enhanced deterrence can result in negotiated, rather than full compliance. The data discussed here are limited, and it is hoped that the ﬁndings derived from them can serve as hypotheses for further study into the interaction among relationship, discretion, and negotiated compliance. Finally, very few studies on decentered regulations have examined the effects of enlisting new actors onto the regulatory landscape on the new actors themselves.
